data.title: Seems investors really don't like tariffs
data.description:
SEEMS INVESTORS REALLY DON'T LIKE TARIFFS

A look at the day ahead in European and global markets from Wayne Cole

The jury is in, and it turns out investors really don't like a tariff-induced global trade war and likely recession - who knew? Wall St futures are down around 3%, as is the Nikkei, while European stock futures are off around 1.7%. Treasury yields hit multi-month lows, while the dollar index hit a near six-month low in pretty chaotic conditions.

The reaction was of a piece with the high drama around the announcement, as President Donald Trump read off various levies on live TV from a big blue and yellow board. Headlining the list was 34% extra for China, 20% on the EU, 32% for Taiwan, 24% for Japan and 46% for Vietnam.

The inclusion of high levies for Asia was clearly a shock to tech shares since it promises to raise costs sharply across their supply chains. Apple shares were down 7% after the bell.

Almost all analysts, contrary to arguments from the White House, see the end of free trade as a shock to U.S. and global economic growth and will be revising up the risk of recession.

That was clear in Fed funds futures which rose to price in 80 basis points of Fed easing this year, even though these tariffs will surely cause U.S. inflation to spike sharply. Analysts are talking of price rises of $6,000 to $10,000 on new autos alone.

Fed officials like to say they will look through a one-off increase in the price level, but the pandemic shows what happens when firms realise they can lift prices and blame it on someone or something else.

It's also not clear how permanent these tariff levels will be, with the White House saying it's open to horse trading with different countries. It's going to be hard for firms to plan long-term investments on such shifting sands.

And then there's the coming countermeasures, as countries line up to defy Trump's new world order. European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen was just on the wires to pledge retaliation should negotiations fail.

As students of history will know, it was the U.S. Smoot-Hawley tariffs in the early 1930s that put the "great" in the "Great Depression".

Trying to keep up with the latest tariff news? Our new daily news digest offers a rundown of the top market-moving headlines impacting global trade. Sign up for Tariff Watch here.

Key developments that could influence markets on Thursday:

- EU producer prices, service PMIs

- US trade data, ISM services, weekly jobless claims. Fed Vice Chair Jefferson and Governor Cook speak

*****

data.title: Australian shares fall as Trump's tariffs roil markets
data.description:
Key points:
  • Miners, energy stocks down more than 2%
  • Investors seek safety in gold, consumer staples
  • US tariffs hit on NZ exporters manageable -analysts

Australian shares closed lower on Thursday as President Donald Trump's unexpectedly aggressive approach to reshape global trade unsettled investors.

The benchmark index S&P/ASX 200 dropped 0.9% to end at 7,859.7 points. It slid as much as 2.1% earlier in the day.

The sell-off was triggered by Trump's overnight announcement of imposing at least a 10% tariff on all exports to the U.S., with even higher duties on some nations to counter large trade imbalances.

"While Australia's direct trade exposure to the U.S. is minimal, the knock-on effects via China and broader Asian nations that have seen hefty tariffs could weigh on our export-heavy economy", said Josh Gilbert, market analyst at eToro.

Citi analysts said, "While reciprocal tariffs may add to domestic inflation, our economists expect the Reserve Bank of Australia could be concerned about consumer confidence, cementing their expectations of 25 basis point rate cuts to occur in May."

In Sydney, miners lost 2.3%. BHP Group , Rio Tinto and Fortescue shed 2.5% to 3.5%.

Banks dropped 0.6%, while energy stocks fell 2.7% as oil prices sank.

Technology stocks ended 2.9% lower, mirroring the overnight drop in Nasdaq futures.

On the flip side, gold stocks gained 1.3%, tracking the jump in bullion prices to a record high.

The hunt for safe havens also pushed investors to consumer staples, which helped the overall consumer index (.AXSJ) gain 1.3%.

"Consumer staples are generally outperformers in nervous markets as investors gravitate towards businesses that have consistent demand ... rather than discretionary spend," said Mark Gardner, CEO and head of equities advisory at MPC Markets.

New Zealand's benchmark S&P/NZX 50 index rose 0.1% to 12,338.57 points.

"The direct impact of U.S. tariffs on New Zealand exporters is likely to be manageable. Indirect effects will be more significant but are harder to assess," Westpac analysts said.

data.title: Bajaj Finance gets RBI's 'letter of displeasure' over lapses in credit card business: CNBC Awaaz
data.description:
Leading NBFC Bajaj Finance has received a 'letter of displeasure' from the banking regulator RBI over lapses in its co-branded credit cards business, stating that the arrangements are exposing customers to risks, CNBC Awaaz reported on April 3, citing people familiar with the development.

The RBI expressed its displeasure over the fact the Bajaj Finance has failed to maintain internal controls and processes for clients, and the NBFC needs to adhere to compliance, while ensuring that there is no conflict of interest for the selection of external auditors in this regard. Bajaj Finance also needs to be undertake data security measures as well as IT and cyber security controls, RBI's letter added.

The Reserve Bank cited a previous letter, dated January 31, to which Bajaj Finance had responded on February 22, in its latest communication, and said that the NBFC has not proactively identified gaps, and had failed to prevent data leakage and fraudulent activities.

Shares of Bajaj Finance are near the day's low, down close to 2% in early trade on April 3.This is being updated

data.title: Tariffs Will Destroy Decades of Integrated Supply Chains, U.K. Manufacturing Body SaysMarket Talk
data.description: The U.K. Government needs to double efforts and work urgently to negotiate a mutually beneficial economic agreement with the U.S. to reverse the tariffs charged on exported goods, chief executive of manufacturing trade body Make UK, Stephen Phipson, says. The tariffs will destroy decades of integrated supply chains connecting the U.K. with U.S. through other trading partners who are also impacted by the tariffs, he says. "This isn't the time for a trade war, we need to find a way to mitigate against these crippling tariffs at speed," Phipson says. (ian.walker@wsj.com.)

data.title: Xi'an Global Printing to Liquidate, Deregister Tech Subsidiary
data.description:
Xi'an Global Printing plans to liquidate and deregister its subsidiary, Xi'an Eblock Digital Technology, as it did not meet performance expectations, according to a Shenzhen Stock Exchange filing on Wednesday.

Eblock Digital Tech is a blockchain services company, the Chinese pharmaceutical packaging products manufacturer said.

Xi'an Global Printing's shares slipped 1% in recent trade.

data.title: xReality Group US Unit Enters Strategic Research Collaboration for High-Stress Law Enforcement Scenarios
data.description:
xReality Group said that its US unit, Operator XR, entered into a strategic research deal with Texas State University's Advanced Law Enforcement Rapid Response Training Center, the FBI-designated national standard for active shooter response training in the US, according to a Thursday Australian bourse filing.

The collaboration has an initial term of two years, with the potential for renewal based on mutual agreement, and the research will optimize Operator XR's technology platform in high-stress law enforcement scenarios.

It will focus on advancing training methodologies using the Operator XR virtual reality training system, the filing said.

Funding arrangements for specific projects will be determined on a case-by-case basis.

Its shares jumped 9% on market close on Thursday.

data.title: South Korea Rushes to Aid Affected Businesses After Trump's Tariff Plans
data.description:
By Kwanwoo Jun

South Korea, in the face of President Trump's tough 25% reciprocal duty, is rushing to aid its auto and other affected businesses, preparing for talks to ease the impact of U.S. tariffs.

Acting President and Prime Minister Han Duck-soo at an economic policy meeting in Seoul on Thursday directed Trade Minister Ahn Duk-guen to prepare "emergency support measures" for the country's auto sector and other industries that will be hit by the U.S. tariffs, according to the trade ministry.

The ministry said it has been closely analyzing the impact of what Seoul thinks were tougher-than-expected U.S. duties on the trade-dependent Asian economy. Trump's announcement followed separate 25% U.S. tariffs on imported vehicles and parts introduced last week.

Han instructed Ahn to scrutinize the reciprocal tariff before actively engaging in follow-up negotiations with the U.S. to minimize industrial damage, according to the ministry.

The ministry said its top trade negotiator will soon visit the U.S. for close consultations with the Trump administration over tariffs.

Citigroup said in a research note Thursday that South Korea could seek a reduction in U.S. tariffs by offering to purchase more U.S. gas, oil, weaponry and commercial airplanes, as well as investing more in the U.S. Seoul could also lower its non-trade barriers and shoulder more of the costs for stationing U.S. troops in South Korea, Citi noted.

The Bank of Korea on Thursday said Trump's tariff measures were stronger than the market had expected. Market volatility could increase going forward, depending on the responses of major U.S. trading partners, a senior Bank of Korea official said.

South Korean financial authorities will closely monitor the situation and take prompt actions, if necessary, to stabilize markets, the BOK official said.

Citigroup recently revised down its 2025 growth forecast for South Korea to 1.0% from 1.2%. Citi economists Jin-Wook Kim and Jiuk Choi expect the BOK to cut the policy rate again in May to support the economy, but they don't rule out an earlier-than-expected cut in April, citing the "sharper-than-expected" U.S. reciprocal tariffs.

Write to Kwanwoo Jun at kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com

data.title: Nikkei on Track for Most Volatile Day Since August 2024Market Talk
data.description: tThe Nikkei Stock Average is on track for its most volatile day since August 2024, says Matt Simpson, market analyst at FOREX.com and City Index, in a commentary. The Nikkei has a day's high-to-low range of negative 9%, the analyst notes. The daily chart shows that momentum is turning lower on the Nikkei once again, Simpson says. "If we're witnessing an ABC retracement, which delivers 'wave equality', it could be headed for the 2021 high," Simpson says. ABC retracement is part of technical analysis called Elliot Wave Theory, where "wave equality" means wave A equals wave C. The Nikkei's 2021 high was 30670.10. The Nikkei Stock Average is last 3.4% down at 34508.82. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0545 GMT - tThe Nikkei Stock Average is on track for its most volatile day since August 2024, says Matt Simpson, market analyst at FOREX.com and City Index, in a commentary. The Nikkei has a day's high-to-low range of negative 9%, the analyst notes. The daily chart shows that momentum is turning lower on the Nikkei once again, Simpson says. "If we're witnessing an ABC retracement, which delivers 'wave equality', it could be headed for the 2021 high," Simpson says. ABC retracement is part of technical analysis called Elliot Wave Theory, where "wave equality" means wave A equals wave C. The Nikkei's 2021 high was 30670.10. The Nikkei Stock Average is last 3.4% down at 34508.82. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0543 GMT - The U.K. Government needs to double efforts and work urgently to negotiate a mutually beneficial economic agreement with the U.S. to reverse the tariffs charged on exported goods, chief executive of manufacturing trade body Make UK, Stephen Phipson, says. The tariffs will destroy decades of integrated supply chains connecting the U.K. with U.S. through other trading partners who are also impacted by the tariffs, he says. "This isn't the time for a trade war, we need to find a way to mitigate against these crippling tariffs at speed," Phipson says. (ian.walker@wsj.com.)

0539 GMT - U.S. tariffs represent a massive burden for auto companies and supply chains and mark a fundamental break in trade policy, says Hildegard Mueller, president of German automotive industry association VDA. Germany exported about 450,000 cars to the U.S. in 2024, according to VDA. The German auto industry also has more than 2,000 sites in the U.S., employing around 138,000 people. By snubbing the global trade order, the U.S. turns away from the basis for value creation and corresponding growth in many regions, Mueller says. "This is not America first, this is America alone," Mueller says. She calls on the EU to present a united front, while signaling its willingness to negotiate. (sarah.sloat@wsj.com)

0537 GMT - Trump's latest tariffs should have limited impact on major Chinese home appliance names, Morningstar analyst Jeff Zhang says.China's appliance makers, such as Midea Group, Gree Electric Appliances and Zhejiang Supor's earnings exposure to the U.S. market should remain below 5%, he says. Haier Smart Home is most vulnerable to tariff hikes as 20%-25% of its profit comes from the U.S. and the analyst expects it to increase production capacities in the U.S. or Mexico. With reciprocal tariffs also applied to Asean countries like Vietnam, Morningstar thinks manufacturers will need to either ramp up capacities in North America or reduce exposure to the U.S. market. As most Chinese appliance makers see faster sales growth in emerging markets, the second option should be easier to adopt, he adds. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0535 GMT - The Trump administration's 10% tariff on U.S. imports of British goods, along with a 25% tariff on vehicles, dashes any hopes that the U.K. would avoid being involved in a trade war, the Institute of Directors says in a note. The tariffs represent another barrier to growth as they will further add pressures to U.K. companies, the organization of British business leaders says. Exporters will have to review the viability of the U.S. as a destination and as a supply chain location, or may have to cut their profit margins to remain competitive, the group adds. The U.S. is the U.K.'s largest trading partner and a key export market for the British industries, particularly the automotive, pharmaceutical, chemicals and whisky sectors, the IoD says. (michael.susin@wsj.com)

0530 GMT - China is likely to escalate its retaliation for the latest U.S. tariffs, although that will inevitably come with supply chain disruptions and further strains on trade relations, Pepperstone research strategist Dilin Wu writes in a note. As tensions escalate, China will need to strengthen ties with alternative trading partners and step up stimulus measures for domestic consumption, Wu adds. If Chinese manufacturers can seize new opportunities and China deepens trade cooperation with the EU and other Asian economies, some related assets could emerge more resilient amid the shifting global trade landscape, Wu says. Investors are closely watching for Beijing's next moves with China's current counter-tariffs set at a relatively moderate 10% to 15%. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0523 GMT - Trade organization spiritsEurope says it supports any efforts made by the European Union to reach a negotiated solution with the U.S. as the spirits sector braces for the tariffs announced by President Trump. The industry is highly integrated across the Atlantic, spiritsEurope says, making the sector particularly vulnerable to tariffs. The association, which represents the sector in the EU, says it is prepared to contribute with a proposal to improve the fortunes of the industry on both sides of the Atlantic."We want to ensure a return to an environment that supports continued growth for both EU and U.S. producers," the association adds. (michael.susin@wsj.com)

0518 GMT - New U.S. tariffs mean U.K. car manufacturers might have to review their output in the face of constrained demand, U.K. industry body Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders said. U.K. products exported to the U.S. have been slapped with a 10% tariff, which is another deeply disappointing and potentially damaging measure, SMMT Chief Executive Mike Hawes says. British cars were already set to attract a 25% tariff and other automotive products are now set to be impacted immediately. "While we hope a deal between the U.K. and U.S. can still be negotiated, this is yet another challenge to a sector already facing multiple headwinds." The tariff costs can't be absorbed by manufacturers, so U.S. consumers may face additional costs and a reduced choice of British brands, he adds. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0510 GMT - China's export-driven companies will face challenges after President Trump's "Liberation Day" tariffs, either in the form of lower margins or reduced sales volumes if they pass on the additional costs to customers, Morningstar analyst Ivan Su says. Weaker exports could further slow China's economic growth and in turn dampen domestic consumption, he adds. "This broader economic slowdown could create a ripple effect, ultimately impacting even domestically-oriented companies," he adds. Su notes that the key factor is how much additional stimulus the Chinese government is prepared to implement to offset the tariff-induced economic slowdown. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Europe Faces Weaker Growth as Tariffs Take TollMarket Talk
data.description: A 20% tariff on all exports to the U.S. is a major blow to the European economy, economists at Dutch bank ING warn in a note, trimming their forecasts for eurozone growth ahead. President Trump on Wednesday set out duties on European imports as part of a major package of tariffs on global goods aiming, he says, to protect domestic manufacture. In Europe, the consequences will hinge much on counter-measures but will certainly in the near term be depressive for growth, ING says. There are bright spots ahead for the continent's economies from planned stimulus, but that will take time, the bank says. As such, the 20 members of the eurozone are likely to book GDP growth of just 0.6% this year and 1.0% in 2026, down from projections of 0.7% and 1.4%, respectively, ING says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

data.title: Haypp Group presents new mid-term financial targets
data.description:
At today's Capital Markets Day in Stockholm, CEO Gavin O’Dowd and members of the Group Management Team will provide further insights into Haypp Group’s growth plans, expected developments in its business environment, and regulatory outlook, as well as present new Financial Targets.

Haypp Group's Financial Targets 2028

The Board of Haypp Group has established the following Financial Targets for 2028:

Sales – Revenue Growth range of 18-25 per cent annually

Profitability – Adjusted EBIT margin of 5.5 percent +/- 150 basis points

Dividend policy

The Board of Haypp Group expects to reinvest cash flows into the company’s continued expansion.

“We had, and will, always put the consumer first. We engage with them every day to understand their needs and desires, and we know them like no other actor in the industry. Our mission is to provide the consumers with the best possible products on the market, while we operate with the highest standards of integrity and business ethics in accordance with applicable regulations”, says Haypp Group’s CEO Gavin O’Dowd.

Previous Financial Targets

Topline Growth – Haypp Group expects to reach net sales of SEK 5 billion by 2025 in its existing business through organic growth only. Expansion into adjacent categories and new markets will be on top.

Profitability – While Haypp Group will continue to prioritise topline growth, it expects to reach a high single digit adjusted EBIT margin in the current Core & Growth Markets business in 2025.

Reinvestment – In order to take advantage of its substantial revenue growth opportunities, and after its successful nicotine vaping pilot in the UK, Haypp Group plans to enter adjacent markets and RRP* categories in Europe, reinvesting approximately 1-2 percentage points of adjusted EBIT margin through 2025.

The event starts at 1:30 pm CEST today. The presentations will be held in English and will be streamed live and made available on Haypp Group’s website and via the following link:

https://app.univid.io/session/67d85919db482eb56f77bba4/

For more information and contact:

Markus Lindblad

Head of External Affairs

+46(0)708153983

markus.lindblad@hayppgroup.com

Erik Bloomquist

Head of Investor Relations

+44(0)7525421916

erik.bloomquist@hayppgroup.com

Certified Advisor

FNCA Sweden AB

* Risk Reduced Products

data.title: Aevis Victoria's FY24 Loss Narrows; Revenue Rises
data.description:
Aevis Victoria said Thursday its loss for the full year 2024 narrowed, while total revenue grew year over year.

Loss attributable to shareholders for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 2.9 million francs, compared with a loss of 39.3 million francs a year ago. Loss per share moved to 0.03 franc from a loss per share of 0.46 franc.

The Swiss investment company's total revenue was 1.06 billion francs, compared with 953 million francs earlier.

data.title: Cosunter Pharmaceutical to Raise 977 Million Yuan From Planned Share Issuance; Shares Down 4%
data.description:
Fujian Cosunter Pharmaceutical plans to raise 976.9 million yuan from a planned share issuance, according to a Shenzhen Stock Exchange filing on Tuesday.

The pharmaceutical company plans to issue 47.8 million shares.

The plan is still subject to the approval of the Chinese securities regulator, the filing said.

The company's shares fell over 4% in recent trade.

data.title: Implenia JV Bags Construction Contract with Deutsche Bahn
data.description:
Implenia said Thursday that its equally held joint venture with infrastructure group Hochtief secured a high-hundred-million-euro contract to work on the eastern section of the Munich S-Bahn railway system in Germany.

Railway company Deutsche Bahn is commissioning the 3-kilometer project, which will involve the construction of structural works for the Ostbahnhof stop and tunnel tubes that will connect Marienhof and Ostbahnhof.

Construction works will begin in June 2025, with completion expected by May 2033, the Swiss construction company noted.

data.title: Market Chatter: Trump Imposes 32% Tariff on Taiwanese Goods
data.description:
US President Donald Trump unveiled new "reciprocal tariffs" on Wednesday, which include a 32% tax on goods from Taiwan, effective April 9, Focus Taiwan reported the same day.

The 10% baseline tax on imports from all countries will take effect on April 5, with higher tariffs for countries with larger trade surpluses with the US. Taiwan, China, Japan, South Korea, Vietnam, and Thailand will face increased duties, ranging from 24% to 46%, according to the publication.

The White House stated that the tariffs are meant to fix trade issues, bring manufacturing back to the US, and boost economic growth. However, experts warn that they could lead to a trade war, higher inflation, and disrupt global trade, the report said.

data.title: Trump's Tariffs Should Have Limited Impact on Chinese Home Appliance MakersMarket Talk
data.description: Trump's latest tariffs should have limited impact on major Chinese home appliance names, Morningstar analyst Jeff Zhang says.China's appliance makers, such as Midea Group, Gree Electric Appliances and Zhejiang Supor's earnings exposure to the U.S. market should remain below 5%, he says. Haier Smart Home is most vulnerable to tariff hikes as 20%-25% of its profit comes from the U.S. and the analyst expects it to increase production capacities in the U.S. or Mexico. With reciprocal tariffs also applied to Asean countries like Vietnam, Morningstar thinks manufacturers will need to either ramp up capacities in North America or reduce exposure to the U.S. market. As most Chinese appliance makers see faster sales growth in emerging markets, the second option should be easier to adopt, he adds. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Business Group CBI Urges U.K. Government to React Calmly to Trump TariffsMarket Talk
data.description: The Confederation of British Industry, a business group, urges the U.K. government to avoid further escalation in its response to President Trump's plan to introduce sweeping tariffs. "A cool and calm reaction from the U.K. Government is the right response: U.K. firms need a measured and proportionate approach which avoids further escalation," CBI CEO Rain Newton-Smith says. "Retaliation will only add to supply chain disruption, slow down investment, and stoke volatility in prices," Newton-Smith says. The U.K. government tried to negotiate a carveout to incoming tariffs through a bilateral economic deal, and negotiating stronger trading relationships with all like-minded partners will be foundational to any success in its efforts to boost economic growth, according to the CBI CEO. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

data.title: Tornadoes, heavy rains rip across central, southern US
data.description:
Tornadoes ripped across a wide swath of central and southern United States on Wednesday, destroying homes and businesses and bringing down power lines and trees.

The National Weather Service said there had been at least 15 reports of tornadoes in at least four states by late Wednesday.

Eight people have been injured across Kentucky and Arkansas, including one critically injured in Kentucky's Ballard County, local officials said.

Late Wednesday, Arkansas Governor Sarah Huckabee Sanders declared a state of emergency across the state due to the storms, which also brought hail and torrential rain.

The NWS said millions of people were under alerts for tornadoes and flash floods and that dangers would continue into early Thursday.

Violent storms are forecast to ravage the country for several days, the NWS said, with Wednesday just "the beginning of a multi-day catastrophic and potentially historic heavy rainfall event."

"The word for tonight is 'chaotic,'" said Scott Kleebauer, a NWS meteorologist. "This is a large expanse of storms migrating slowly to the east, stretching from southeast Michigan down into southeastern Arkansas."

The town of Nevada, Missouri, was hit by a tornado. Writing on social media, the state's Emergency Management Agency said it caused "major damage to several businesses, power poles were snapped and several (empty) train cars were flipped onto their sides by the powerful storm!"

The NWS issued tornado and flash flood warnings for parts of Missouri, Arkansas, Tennessee, Mississippi, Indiana, Illinois, Kentucky and Oklahoma.

It called the rain threats for Arkansas, Missouri, Tennessee and Mississippi in the coming days a "generational flood event" with some locations forecast to see as much as 15 inches (38.1 cm) of rain by the weekend, which could cause rivers to burst their banks and cause "catastrophic river flooding."

More than 400,000 customers had their power knocked out across the storm-hit area, according to PowerOutage.us.

data.title: Tariffs Place Massive Burden on German CarmakersMarket Talk
data.description: U.S. tariffs represent a massive burden for auto companies and supply chains and mark a fundamental break in trade policy, says Hildegard Mueller, president of German automotive industry association VDA. Germany exported about 450,000 cars to the U.S. in 2024, according to VDA. The German auto industry also has more than 2,000 sites in the U.S., employing around 138,000 people. By snubbing the global trade order, the U.S. turns away from the basis for value creation and corresponding growth in many regions, Mueller says. "This is not America first, this is America alone," Mueller says. She calls on the EU to present a united front, while signaling its willingness to negotiate. (sarah.sloat@wsj.com)

data.title: Tariffs Make U.S. Recession, Higher Inflation More LikelyMarket Talk
data.description: Recession and inflation are now more likely, says Franklin Templeton Institute's Stephen Dover in a note. "Tariffs do not work if prices do not increase," the chief market strategist says. The average American family may pay up to an estimated $4,200 more per year because of tariffs, assuming an average 20% tariff rate on imports, Dover says. "Tariffs, therefore, will likely slow household and business spending and we expect them to increase the risk of U.S. growth and earnings disappointments in 2025." (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0139 ET - U.S. tariffs represent a massive burden for auto companies and supply chains and mark a fundamental break in trade policy, says Hildegard Mueller, president of German automotive industry association VDA. Germany exported about 450,000 cars to the U.S. in 2024, according to VDA. The German auto industry also has more than 2,000 sites in the U.S., employing around 138,000 people. By snubbing the global trade order, the U.S. turns away from the basis for value creation and corresponding growth in many regions, Mueller says. "This is not America first, this is America alone," Mueller says. She calls on the EU to present a united front, while signaling its willingness to negotiate. (sarah.sloat@wsj.com)

0137 ET - The Confederation of British Industry, a business group, urges the U.K. government to avoid further escalation in its response to President Trump's plan to introduce sweeping tariffs. "A cool and calm reaction from the U.K. Government is the right response: U.K. firms need a measured and proportionate approach which avoids further escalation," CBI CEO Rain Newton-Smith says. "Retaliation will only add to supply chain disruption, slow down investment, and stoke volatility in prices," Newton-Smith says. The U.K. government tried to negotiate a carveout to incoming tariffs through a bilateral economic deal, and negotiating stronger trading relationships with all like-minded partners will be foundational to any success in its efforts to boost economic growth, according to the CBI CEO. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0135 ET - The Trump administration's 10% tariff on U.S. imports of British goods, along with a 25% tariff on vehicles, dashes any hopes that the U.K. would avoid being involved in a trade war, the Institute of Directors says in a note. The tariffs represent another barrier to growth as they will further add pressures to U.K. companies, the organization of British business leaders says. Exporters will have to review the viability of the U.S. as a destination and as a supply chain location, or may have to cut their profit margins to remain competitive, the group adds. The U.S. is the U.K.'s largest trading partner and a key export market for the British industries, particularly the automotive, pharmaceutical, chemicals and whisky sectors, the IoD says. (michael.susin@wsj.com)

0134 ET - President Trump's tariffs are steeper than might have been expected and will probably spur new cuts to interest rates across the world's developed economies, Capital Economics' Neil Shearing and Paul Ashworth write in a note. A 10% baseline plus tailored duties on individual economies and goods-sectors will make up an effective U.S. tariff rate of around 25% all told, according to Capital's calculations. China will be hit the hardest, Shearing and Ashworth say. But decimal points will also be shaved off growth in the U.K and the eurozone, they say. "Most advanced-economy central banks will probably view this as another reason to cut interest rates," they add. The European Central Bank will decide its next policy steps later in April, and the Bank of England in May. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0123 ET - Trade organization spiritsEurope says it supports any efforts made by the European Union to reach a negotiated solution with the U.S. as the spirits sector braces for the tariffs announced by President Trump. The industry is highly integrated across the Atlantic, spiritsEurope says, making the sector particularly vulnerable to tariffs. The association, which represents the sector in the EU, says it is prepared to contribute with a proposal to improve the fortunes of the industry on both sides of the Atlantic."We want to ensure a return to an environment that supports continued growth for both EU and U.S. producers," the association adds. (michael.susin@wsj.com)

0104 ET - Spain will keep fighting for an open world, premier Pedro Sanchez insists as the EU braces for 20% tariffs on its exports to the U.S., the bloc's most important trading partner. The trade duties, part of a major package set out Wednesday by President Trump, could hamstring exporters and depress Europe's growth. "Spain will protect its companies and its workers and will continue to back openness in the world," Sanchez says on X following a meeting with employer and worker associations.(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

2232 ET - The sweeping Trump tariffs could pose higher-than-expected risks to China's exports, says Pinpoint Asset Management chief economist Zhiwei Zhang in a note. The high tariffs on Asean countries, such as Vietnam and Cambodia, could weigh on China's exports to these countries, he says. Supply chains in East Asia are vertically integrated as China's exports of intermediate goods are crucial for these countries' exports, he says. "Retaliation from China on the U.S. tariffs is likely coming," he says. Zhang expects Beijing to push up fiscal spending and cut interest rate and reserve requirement ratio to offset the damage to China's exports, he says. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2100 ET - NSW Farmers, which represents producers in Australia's most populous state of New South Wales, urged Australia to invest in new markets after the U.S. imposed a 10% reciprocal tariff on Australian imports. Trump mentioned Australian beef in his speech announcing the tariffs, saying that Australia doesn't import American beef but that the U.S. imports a lot of Australian beef. NSW Farmers say Australian produce is in demand in China, Korea and Japan, and there are growth opportunities in the Middle East and North Africa. The group, however, stood by the Australian government's decision not to retaliate, with NSW Farmers President Xavier Martin saying that "a tit-for-tat approach with the U.S. serves no purpose." (mike.cherney@wsj.com; @Mike_Cherney)

2033 ET - AUD​/USD ​has ranged through a high ​of 0.6341​ to a low of low of 0.6226​ since U.S. President Trump announced a sweeping new tariff regime on his country's trading partners. The pair now trades at 0.6259. It amounts to only a modest net decline for the ​AUD, with Australia escaping the tariff hike relatively lightly, and with lots of bad news already priced into the currency​, says Sean Callow, currency analyst at InTouch Capital Markets. Traders will be bracing for China's response​ through the USD/CNY fix, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

2010 ET - Copper has escaped a tariff on sales into the U.S.--at least for now. Copper and bullion, among some other products, will not be subject to President Trump's reciprocal tariff plan, according to the White House. Still, that could be because it will likely face its own tariff later, says AMP's Shane Oliver. Last month, Trump threatened to impose a duty of as much as 25% on imported copper. The U.S. mostly imports copper from Chile, Canada and Peru. Steel and aluminum, already subject to a 25% tariff, were also on the exemption list. LME 3-month copper falls by 1.2%, amid concerns about the impact of tariffs on global growth. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1936 ET - President Trump's reciprocal tariff on major appliance manufacturing countries, including China, Vietnam, and Thailand, is likely to lead to retailers raising platform fees by another 30%, according to Jefferies analysts in a note. Unlike China, the U.S. appliance market is dominated by large chain stores such as Home Depot, Best Buy, and Amazon. The note states that with these platforms increasing their fees, brands' operations in the U.S. would become largely unprofitable. (venkat.pr@wsj.com)

1933 ET - Trump's reciprocal tariff on major textile and footwear manufacturing nations will place original equipment manufacturers under significant margin and price pressure over the next 3-5 years, Jefferies says in a note. Vietnam is likely to lose its status as the leading exporter of sporting goods to the U.S. At the same time, new capacity is expected to be added in low-cost countries like Indonesia, India, and in Africa. Surging capital expenditures and margin pressure may result in cash burn for the OEMs, it adds. (venkat.pr@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0539 GMT - U.S. tariffs represent a massive burden for auto companies and supply chains and mark a fundamental break in trade policy, says Hildegard Mueller, president of German automotive industry association VDA. Germany exported about 450,000 cars to the U.S. in 2024, according to VDA. The German auto industry also has more than 2,000 sites in the U.S., employing around 138,000 people. By snubbing the global trade order, the U.S. turns away from the basis for value creation and corresponding growth in many regions, Mueller says. "This is not America first, this is America alone," Mueller says. She calls on the EU to present a united front, while signaling its willingness to negotiate. (sarah.sloat@wsj.com)

0537 GMT - The Confederation of British Industry, a business group, urges the U.K. government to avoid further escalation in its response to President Trump's plan to introduce sweeping tariffs. "A cool and calm reaction from the U.K. Government is the right response: U.K. firms need a measured and proportionate approach which avoids further escalation," CBI CEO Rain Newton-Smith says. "Retaliation will only add to supply chain disruption, slow down investment, and stoke volatility in prices," Newton-Smith says. The U.K. government tried to negotiate a carveout to incoming tariffs through a bilateral economic deal, and negotiating stronger trading relationships with all like-minded partners will be foundational to any success in its efforts to boost economic growth, according to the CBI CEO. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0536 GMT - Asian equities could be weighed in the short term by steeper-than-expected sweeping U.S. tariffs, says James Ooi, a market strategist at Tiger Brokers. Tariffs could dampen the regional growth outlook and raise the risk of retaliatory tariffs from affected Asian countries, Ooi writes. The Asia-Pacific region, characterized by its export-driven economies, is likely to face headwinds if global trade activity slows, Ooi says. Chinese-owned factories could also need to reconsider their relocation strategies as Southeast Asian countries such as Vietnam and Cambodia are subject to higher tariffs, Ooi adds. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0535 GMT - The Trump administration's 10% tariff on U.S. imports of British goods, along with a 25% tariff on vehicles, dashes any hopes that the U.K. would avoid being involved in a trade war, the Institute of Directors says in a note. The tariffs represent another barrier to growth as they will further add pressures to U.K. companies, the organization of British business leaders says. Exporters will have to review the viability of the U.S. as a destination and as a supply chain location, or may have to cut their profit margins to remain competitive, the group adds. The U.S. is the U.K.'s largest trading partner and a key export market for the British industries, particularly the automotive, pharmaceutical, chemicals and whisky sectors, the IoD says. (michael.susin@wsj.com)

0534 GMT - President Trump's tariffs are steeper than might have been expected and will probably spur new cuts to interest rates across the world's developed economies, Capital Economics' Neil Shearing and Paul Ashworth write in a note. A 10% baseline plus tailored duties on individual economies and goods-sectors will make up an effective U.S. tariff rate of around 25% all told, according to Capital's calculations. China will be hit the hardest, Shearing and Ashworth say. But decimal points will also be shaved off growth in the U.K and the eurozone, they say. "Most advanced-economy central banks will probably view this as another reason to cut interest rates," they add. The European Central Bank will decide its next policy steps later in April, and the Bank of England in May. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0534 GMT - U.S. Treasury prices rallied in the wake of President Trump's tariff announcements, with yields sliding across maturities, driven by the five- to 10-year maturities, according to Tradeweb data. "This morning global bond yields are declining as the tariffs are seen to be much more aggressive than expected and raising the fear of a recession," says Danske Bank Research's chief analyst Stefan Mellin in a note. The two-year Treasury yield falls 10.5 basis points to last trade at 3.799%; the five-year Treasury yield falls 14.5 basis points to 3.807%; the 10-year Treasury yield slides almost 14 basis points to 4.059%. The 30-year Treasury yield drops 10.5 basis points to 4.446%. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0530 GMT - China is likely to escalate its retaliation for the latest U.S. tariffs, although that will inevitably come with supply chain disruptions and further strains on trade relations, Pepperstone research strategist Dilin Wu writes in a note. As tensions escalate, China will need to strengthen ties with alternative trading partners and step up stimulus measures for domestic consumption, Wu adds. If Chinese manufacturers can seize new opportunities and China deepens trade cooperation with the EU and other Asian economies, some related assets could emerge more resilient amid the shifting global trade landscape, Wu says. Investors are closely watching for Beijing's next moves with China's current counter-tariffs set at a relatively moderate 10% to 15%. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0528 GMT - Swedish Prime Minister Ulf Kristersson said U.S tariffs will make Americans and Swedes poorer and the world more dangerous. Mutual cooperation through free trade has contributed to peace, technological progress and reduced poverty globally, he says. "It is free enterprise and free competition that have laid the foundation for the prosperity of the west. That is why Americans can listen to music on Swedish Spotify and we Swedes can listen to the same music on American iPhones." However, Sweden is well prepared for what is happening, with a stable economic footing and "world-class" government finances, he adds. "We want to find a way together with the U.S. where we trade and cooperate so that people in our countries are better off." (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0523 GMT - Trade organization spiritsEurope says it supports any efforts made by the European Union to reach a negotiated solution with the U.S. as the spirits sector braces for the tariffs announced by President Trump. The industry is highly integrated across the Atlantic, spiritsEurope says, making the sector particularly vulnerable to tariffs. The association, which represents the sector in the EU, says it is prepared to contribute with a proposal to improve the fortunes of the industry on both sides of the Atlantic."We want to ensure a return to an environment that supports continued growth for both EU and U.S. producers," the association adds. (michael.susin@wsj.com)

0521 GMT - U.S. President Trump's reciprocal tariffs make central banks' interest-rate cuts difficult, says Pepperstone's Michael Brown in a note. The tariffs announced Wednesday are "the punchier/most bearish end of expectations, with high levies, and little time to negotiate them away before implementation," the senior research strategist says. "Good luck getting any rate cuts amid the macro mess that lot will cause!" Brown says that 'Liberation Day' seems much more likely to be the beginning of a tit-for-tat trade war across the globe, rather than a clearing event that brings the issue of tariffs to an end from a market and macro perspective. The U.S. will impose a 10% tariff on all imports and much higher rates for some nations, according to Trump's announcement. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0518 GMT - The latest tariffs announced by U.S. President Trump marks a sharp escalation of his trade war, Vasu Menon, OCBC managing director of investment strategy writes in a note. Although sectoral tariffs on copper, semiconductors, pharmaceuticals and lumber products were not in focus, they could still be hit with tariffs in coming months, he says. But the wait for Trump's biggest trade salvo--reciprocal tariffs--is over, he says. The anticipation of what Trump could do had caused a great deal of uncertainty, he notes. "Nevertheless, markets will need time to internalize and assess Trump's latest tariff bazooka and this along with the risk of retaliation from trading partners will keep investors on edge and nervous for now," he says.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0513 GMT - China is likely to start bolstering trade defenses ahead of negotiations, Macquarie analyst Eugene Hsiao writes in a note. Trump's new tariff announcements signal that now is the real starting point for trade negotiations, he says. While the potential shock to China's economy has already been widely discussed, markets remain in a holding pattern on discounting the real impact to growth until more clarity is reached, he says. China's recent tech re-rating is mostly insulated from tariffs, he says, adding that investment focus now returns to potential domestic stimulus to boost consumption to offset tariff impact. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: European Markets Poised for Sharp Losses
data.description:
European equity markets were set to open sharply lower on Thursday as investors reacted to US President Donald Trump’s aggressive tariff measures.

Trump introduced a 10% baseline tariff on all imports, including a 20% levy on goods from the European Union.

Markets will also closely watch European automakers, as Trump’s 25% tariff on imported cars takes immediate effect.

In response, European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen warned that the EU is preparing countermeasures against the US if negotiations fail.

Beyond trade tensions, investors will assess the latest manufacturing and services PMI reports for further insight into the region’s economic outlook.

In premarket trade, Euro Stoxx 50 and Stoxx 600 futures were down about 2%.

data.title: Holcim Acquires Peru's Compañía Minera Luren
data.description:
Swiss building materials company Holcim acquired Peru-based Compañía Minera Luren.

The acquired company provides walling systems and other specialty building materials, according to a Thursday release. Holcim said the acquisition is highly complementary to its existing business in Peru.
